title: System Design Foundations
summary: A concise introduction to system design principles, trade-offs, and real-world examples.

lessons.title: What is System Design
lessons.content:
# What is System Design
System design is the practice of defining the architecture, components, services, interfaces, and data models that enable a software system to satisfy functional and non-functional requirements. It focuses on high-level structure, interactions, and trade-offs rather than low-level implementation details.
## Scope and goals
- Functional requirements: what the system must do.
- Non-functional requirements: performance, scalability, reliability, security, maintainability, and cost.
## Key building blocks
- Clients and APIs: how users and other systems interact with your service.
- Services and components: logical boundaries and responsibilities.
- Data storage: databases, caches, and their consistency models.
- Messaging and integration: queues, streams, and asynchronous patterns.
- Infrastructure: load balancers, CDNs, replication, and deployment topology.
## A structured approach
1. Clarify requirements and constraints (scale, latency, consistency).
2. Estimate expected load, data volume, and growth patterns.
3. Propose a high-level architecture with clear component responsibilities.
4. Dive into critical components (data modeling, APIs, caching, storage).
5. Outline failure modes, monitoring, and operational procedures.
## Short example: URL shortener
Requirements: create short links, redirect quickly, support high read volume, store mappings persistently, and track basic analytics.
Architecture: API service, datastore for mappings, caching layer for hot keys, and background workers for analytics.
Considerations: key generation and collisions, cache invalidation, scaling reads, and data backup.
## Learning outcomes
- Break down a problem into components and justify architectural choices.
- Reason about trade-offs between latency, consistency, and cost.
- Identify operational needs: monitoring, alerts, backups, and recovery.
**Next:** Read 'Common Trade-offs in System Design' to explore concrete decision examples.

title: Back-of-the-Envelope Estimation
summary: Quick, interview-friendly estimation techniques and exercises for capacity and latency planning.

lessons.title: Overview: What is Back-of-the-Envelope Estimation
lessons.content:
# Back-of-the-Envelope Estimation

Back-of-the-envelope (BOTE) estimation is a compact technique for getting order-of-magnitude answers quickly. It combines straightforward arithmetic, explicit assumptions, and a few well-known performance numbers to decide whether a design approach is viable. The emphasis is on the process and assumptions rather than exact precision.

In interviews and early designs you should: list assumptions, choose round numbers, and label units. This module provides the small reference tables you'll use for quick calculations.

lessons.title: Units & Power-of-Two (data sizes)
lessons.content:
# Units & Power-of-Two (data sizes)

A byte is 8 bits. Use binary powers for precise magnitudes and decimal shortcuts for easy math.

| Power | Approx. value | Full name | Short name |
| -----: | -------------: | --------- | ---------- |
| 2^10 | ~1 thousand | 1 Kilobyte | 1 KB |
| 2^20 | ~1 million | 1 Megabyte | 1 MB |
| 2^30 | ~1 billion | 1 Gigabyte | 1 GB |
| 2^40 | ~1 trillion | 1 Terabyte | 1 TB |
| 2^50 | ~1 quadrillion | 1 Petabyte | 1 PB |

Notes: for quick mental arithmetic you can use 1 KB≈10^3, 1 MB≈10^6, but always write the units to avoid confusion.

lessons.title: Latency Numbers Every Engineer Should Know
lessons.content:
# Latency Numbers & Rules of Thumb

Keep a compact table of typical latencies so you can compare design choices quickly. These are order-of-magnitude guides, not precise benchmarks.

| Operation | Typical time |
| --------- | ------------: |
| L1 cache reference | ~1 ns |
| Branch mispredict | ~5 ns |
| L2 cache reference | ~3–7 ns |
| Mutex lock/unlock | ~100 ns |
| Main memory (DRAM) | ~100 ns |
| Compress 1 KB (fast) | ~2–10 µs |
| Send 2 KB over 1 Gbps | ~20 µs |
| Read 1 MB sequential from memory | ~250 µs |
| SSD random read | ~50–100 µs |
| Datacenter round-trip | ~0.5–2 ms |
| Read 1 MB from network/disk | ~10–30 ms |
| Disk seek (HDD) | ~5–10 ms |
| Intercontinental RTT | ~100–200 ms |

Use these to decide when to cache, when to avoid disk seeks, and when network hops matter.

lessons.title: Availability & the 'Nines'
lessons.content:
# Availability (the 'Nines')

Availability targets and expected downtime per year: choose the right target based on user impact and cost.

| Availability | Downtime per year |
| ------------ | -----------------: |
| 99% | ~3.65 days |
| 99.9% | ~8.76 hours |
| 99.99% | ~52.56 minutes |
| 99.999% | ~5.26 minutes |
| 99.9999% | ~31.54 seconds |

Higher availability requires redundancy, automation, and usually more cost and operational complexity.

lessons.title: Worked Example: Estimating QPS & Storage
lessons.content:
# Worked Example: QPS and Storage

**Assumptions (example)**
- Monthly active users (MAU): 50,000,000
- Fraction active daily: 30% → DAU = 15,000,000
- Uploads (posts) per active user per day: 1.5
- Fraction with media: 30%
- Average media size: 2 MB
- Retention: 3 years

**Step 1 — Baseline QPS**
Posts/sec = DAU * posts_per_day / 86,400
= 15,000,000 * 1.5 / 86,400 ≈ 260.4 ≈ 260 QPS

**Step 2 — Peak QPS**
Apply headroom (2–3×) for spikes: peak ≈ 520–780 QPS

**Step 3 — Media storage**
Daily media = DAU * posts_per_day * media_fraction * avg_media_size
= 15,000,000 * 1.5 * 0.3 * 2 MB = 13.5 TB/day
3-year media storage ≈ 13.5 TB * 365 * 3 ≈ 14.8 PB

**Discussion**
Media fraction and retention dominate storage; small changes to these assumptions have large impacts. Round values for quick reasoning and always state units.

lessons.title: Practical Tips & Best Practices
lessons.content:
# Practical Tips for BOTE

- Round early and keep arithmetic simple.
- Write assumptions and label units.
- Work in orders of magnitude and highlight sensitivities.
- When time is limited, state steps and assumptions before computing.
- Practice common calculations: QPS, storage, caches, and server counts.

lessons.title: Exercises: Practice Problems
lessons.content:
# Exercises

Use the fields below to enter your answers. Click 'Show hint' for help. You can enter numeric answers (units optional).

assumptions: Uploads (posts) per active user per day = 1.5

questions.prompt: Photo app — estimate daily media (in TB/day).
questions.hint: DAU = MAU * daily_fraction = 50,000,000 * 0.3 = 15,000,000. Daily media = DAU * posts_per_day * media_fraction * avg_media_size.

questions.prompt: Photo app — estimate 3-year media storage (in PB).
questions.hint: 3-year storage = daily_media * 365 * 3 and convert to PB (1 PB = 1000 TB for rough BOTE).

questions.prompt: Servers — peak 10k QPS, server handles 500 RPS at peak; compute server count with 20% buffer.
questions.hint: Base servers = peak / per_server; apply buffer by multiplying by 1.2, then round up.

questions.prompt: Cache — 1B total objects, top 1% hottest, avg object 2 KB — estimate cache size (in GB) to hold the hottest 1%.
questions.hint: Hot objects = total * 0.01. Size = hot_objects * avg_object_size. Convert to GB (approx 1 GB = 1,000,000 KB for rough math).
